description:
You are on-call as an operations engineer who must confirm that the host can still
reach minimum performance requirements after a high-CPU incident.

1. Create the directory
   /home/user/benchmark_logs
   if it does not already exist.

2. Launch three short *but concurrent* performance benchmarks:

   • A CPU benchmark that runs for roughly 5 s  
   • A memory-throughput benchmark that runs for roughly 5 s  
   • A disk-I/O benchmark that runs for roughly 5 s (use a temporary test file in
     /home/user/benchmark_logs so you stay in the same writable area)

   You may use any standard CLI benchmarking tool that is already present in the
   container (for example, `sysbench`, `stress`, `fio`, etc.) or simple shell
   constructs as long as the commands really execute in parallel (e.g. via “&” or
   `parallel`).  Do **not** run them sequentially.

3. Just **before** launching each individual test, capture its start time in
   UNIX epoch seconds (`date +%s`).  When the test finishes, capture its end
   time and its numeric exit status.

4. Immediately append one line for that test to the log file
   /home/user/benchmark_logs/incident_2023_001.log
   using the exact pipe-delimited format below (no extra spaces, tabs or blank
   lines, one line per test, order unimportant):

      CPU|<start_epoch>|<end_epoch>|<exit_code>
      MEM|<start_epoch>|<end_epoch>|<exit_code>
      DISK|<start_epoch>|<end_epoch>|<exit_code>

   Requirements for the log file:
   • It must end with a single trailing newline.  
   • Each “epoch” field must be a *10-digit* positive integer.  
   • The exit_code field must be exactly “0” for all three lines.  
   • Nothing else (headers, comments, extra columns, extra whitespace) should be
     present in this file.

5. After all three tests have completed, verify that every recorded
   exit_code is 0.  If—**and only if**—all three exit codes are zero, create
   the file

      /home/user/benchmark_logs/summary.txt

   whose entire content is exactly the single line

      ALL_TESTS_PASSED

   followed by a newline.

6. Do **not** remove any files you created; the automated grader will inspect
   them.  Make sure the permissions you leave allow a non-root process to read
   every file inside /home/user/benchmark_logs.

Hints (not mandatory):  
• `sysbench --test=cpu --time=5 run` is available in most base images.  
• You can run the three commands in background using “&” and wait for them
  collectively with `wait`.  
• Use `echo "CPU|$START|$END|$RC" >> …` to append a properly formatted line.

When you are done, the grader will check:

• That the directory and both files exist,  
• That the log file contains exactly three correctly formatted lines matching
  the specification above, and  
• That summary.txt exists and contains exactly “ALL_TESTS_PASSED\n”.
truth:
The automated tests will verify the following:

1. Directory existence  
   Path: /home/user/benchmark_logs  
   Must exist and be a directory.

2. Log file existence  
   Path: /home/user/benchmark_logs/incident_2023_001.log  
   Must exist and be a regular file.

3. Log file content validation  
   • File must contain **exactly three** newline-terminated lines.  
   • Each line must match the regular expression  
     ^(CPU|MEM|DISK)\|[0-9]{10}\|[0-9]{10}\|0$  
   • The three “test-name” tokens (CPU, MEM, DISK) must all appear once,
     order irrelevant.

4. Summary file existence & content  
   Path: /home/user/benchmark_logs/summary.txt  
   Must exist and be a regular file.  
   Its content (byte-for-byte) must be the 15-byte string:  
   “ALL_TESTS_PASSED\n”

No other files inside /home/user/benchmark_logs will be checked, and timing
information is not required to match any pre-defined value as long as it
satisfies the pattern above.
